story:
Desperate for work, I took a job attending funerals. Not as goulash as it sounds. I opened the church a couple of hours before the service and locked it long after the last guest drifted out. The minister handled the preaching and the comforting, but he couldn’t be expected to stand guard all day. I was there five hours, easy. The pay was decent enough.

People say dead men tell no tales. They do. And those who attend them tell even more. A life’s story leaks out at the seams of a funeral. The obituary calls him a faithful husband, but why is the girlfriend on the aisle across from the wife? If he was a loving father, where is the oldest daughter, and why won’t anyone say her name? If he was a good husband, why does the widow look like she’s set down a heavy suitcase at last? Maybe not a murderer’s grin—more the quiet relief of someone who won’t be dragged anymore.

The body speaks, too. It’s right there, proof and witness. Illness or age sands away the edges of a person—the weight, the strength, the spark. Gaunt faces. Sunken eyes. Lips tugged thin even before they’re sewn. Old, wrinkled fingers that squeezed pennies too tight or let chances slip through them. You can read a kind of history in the stillness.

People want to talk at funerals, especially to the stranger who doesn’t know the backstory. That way it feels like news instead of gossip. And if I judge, so what? I’m just the guy watching the punch bowl, waiting for it to go empty so the family can go home and live with what they said out loud. One person recites every accomplishment like a resume pinned to a wreath. Sooner or later, another leans in and offers the rest—the missing lines. The truth hides between words the way a house ad hides the mold: cozy means small, fixer-upper means trouble.

She loved children, they say, because she had none, and no one mentions the doctors, the prayers, the quiet ache. He railed against abortion, loud and righteous, maybe because that one decision long ago—one he pushed someone into—kept knocking on the door of his sleep. He gave generously to every good cause but skimmed from his partner when no one was looking. She “loved life,” the program says, and yet she took her own. He will be buried beside the wife he was expected to marry instead of the man he loved; the times and the family did their forcing. They will share a headstone the size of a small northern state: beloved wife; beloved husband, carved deep in stone, as if depth makes it truer.

I’m afraid of my own funeral. Not the dying. The telling. I worry about the stranger in the back—some version of me—hearing the secrets I hid behind polite sentences and clean shirts. I can see him already, waiting for the punch to run out, hands on the keys, ears open. Who does he think he is?
